entry.title: Exclude condition types from Profit Margin Calculation
entry.content:
In a pricing procedure, how can I exclude specific condition types from the profit margin calculation?

I have some surcharge condition types set up in my pricing procedure, which the customer needs to pay for, but these conditions must not affect the profit margin calculation for PCIP, is there a way to achieve this?

entry.title: Re: Say it straight
entry.content: I make sense of messy, complex data and turn it into stories that spark insight and help people make smarter decisions.

entry.title: Custom Joule Agent for SAP Ariba SLP - Building an AI Agent for Supplier Registration Approval
entry.content:
The Premise

I'll be honest — I've built this exact workflow before.

In previous posts, I put together a supplier registration approval automation using SAP Build Process Automation. It worked, but it took me a significant amount of time to complete: process flows, variables, connections, data mappings, error handling — each piece designed and wired manually. You can check out those articles Extending SLP functionalities with SAP BTP and Artificial Intelligence  and SAP Ariba SLP - Optical character recognition and Extraction for Supplier Registration Process .

This time, I completed a fully functional proof of concept in less than 7 days.

Same business problem. Same outcome. A fraction of the effort.

Going in, I had all the questions a developer instinctively asks: Who manages the data and how? Where do variables live? How are connections handled? What happens when something fails? These are the questions that typically consume the first week of any integration project before a single line of logic is written.

With Joule Studio, most of those questions answered themselves. The agent managed context between steps, retained the values I needed across the workflow, and handled the SAP Ariba skill connections natively — often without me explicitly instructing it to. The infrastructure just disappeared.

What remained was the part that actually matters: defining the business logic, the scoring model, the decision rules, and the communication to the supplier. The work that requires domain knowledge, not plumbing.

The Real Cost of Manual Supplier Review

Every supplier onboarding in SAP Ariba SLP follows the same predictable path. The supplier fills out a registration questionnaire. An approval task lands in someone's queue. That someone opens it, reads through the answers, checks the numbers, looks for the documents, and makes a call: approve, send it back, or reject.

Sounds simple. It really doesn't feel that way at month-end when the queue has 40 tasks in it.

Here's what always got me: most of what a reviewer is doing in that queue isn't actually a judgment call. They're reading a free-text answer asking themselves — does this BCP response mention an RPO? Does this AML section describe a whistleblowing channel? They're checking if a growth rate in one field matches a balance change in another. They're confirming a document is attached. That's not analysis. That's a checklist. A very slow, very manual, very inconsistent checklist being run by people whose time is worth a lot more than that.

And the problems that came from it were real:

  • Reviews varied wildly depending on who did them — the same questionnaire could sail through with one reviewer and generate three rounds of back-and-forth with another, often over a misspelled word or an answer that was technically correct but phrased oddly.
  • Tasks just sat there — not because nobody cared, but because reviewers had other priorities and supplier registration wasn't always the urgent thing. Suppliers would wait days on submissions that should have been a five-minute review.
  • Nothing was documented — approvals went through with no written rationale. The moment someone asked "why was this supplier approved?" the answer was essentially "because someone clicked approve."
  • Language became a routing problem — suppliers in Argentina or Brazil submitting in Spanish or Portuguese meant we couldn't just assign the task to anyone. It either waited for the right person or got reviewed by someone who couldn't properly read the answers.

None of these are hard problems. They're consistency problems. And the fix isn't hiring more reviewers — it's stopping the pattern-matching work from reaching a human in the first place.

That's where this started.

How it works?

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

How it looks?

 

How can I build my own?

Before anything else, you'll need to set up Joule Studio inside SAP Build. If you haven't done that yet, this guide walks you through it - How to get Started with Joule Studio 

Once you're in, you'll notice two new options in the Build lobby: Create a Joule Agent and Create a Joule Skill. Think of skills as the hands of the agent — they are the individual actions it can take. The agent is the brain that decides when and how to use them.

For this project, we built 4 custom skills, each one mapped to a specific SAP Ariba API call:

SkillWhat It Does
getPendingTasksRetrieves all active approval tasks from SAP Ariba SLP
getTaskDetailsFetches full task data including document ID, URL, and approval chain
getQuestionsAnswersPulls the supplier's questionnaire responses by document ID
postDecisionPosts the approval decision and comment back to the task in Ariba

These four skills are the only connection points between the agent and SAP Ariba. Everything else — the analysis, the scoring, the decision logic, the multilingual handling — lives entirely inside the agent configuration. No additional middleware, no custom code.

In Joule Studio, the foundation of every skill is the destination and the action endpoint. This is fundamentally different from how SAP Build Process Automation handles connectivity. In BPA, you could get away with loosely configured connections and patch things as you went. In Joule Studio, that doesn't work. Each skill needs a correctly defined destination in SAP BTP — the right authentication, the right base URL, the right headers — and each action within that skill needs a precisely mapped endpoint. Get either of those wrong and the skill fails silently, or worse, returns data you can't trust.

Check the SAP BTP Destinations section, it will give you the base for setting Destinations - End to End guide to setup Joule, SAP Build Process Automation, and SAP Build Work Zone on SAP BTP 

Let's make this concrete. Below is the actual configuration of getPendingTasks — the first skill the agent calls every time it runs. On the left you can see the destination binding pointing to the SAP Ariba environment. Below it, the action defines the specific endpoint the skill hits, the HTTP method, and the parameters it passes. This is the complete bridge between Joule Studio and Ariba. No code, no transformation layer — just a correctly configured destination and a precisely defined action

getPendingTasks Joule Skill

Pending Task Destination Response

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

What about the AI Agent?

Once your skills are ready, you create the agent. Joule Studio gives you three sections to configure. Each one has a distinct job — and understanding the boundary between them is what makes the difference between an agent that works reliably and one that kind of works most of the time.

 

Expertise — Who the agent is

This is where you define the agent's identity, role, and domain knowledge. Not what it does step by step, but what it knows going in. Think of it as the briefing you'd give a new analyst before their first day on the job.

In our case, we defined the agent as Joule, embedded in SAP Ariba SLP, with deep knowledge of supplier onboarding workflows, approval mechanics, questionnaire interpretation, and the SLP object model. We spelled out the domain concepts — tasks, workspaces, approval requests, registration projects — so the agent could interpret ambiguous API data through the right lens. When a financial number looked inconsistent, it needed procurement context to judge it correctly. This section provided that foundation.

Instructions — What the agent does

This is the behavioral core. A sequential set of rules that tells the agent exactly how to act, in what order, and under what conditions. Every step, every trigger, every output format lives here.

In our case, this section contained the full seven-step workflow: filtering tasks, building the results table, triggering the questionnaire analysis on a specific user command, running the three-part scoring analysis automatically, and posting the decision without waiting for confirmation. We also defined the comment templates for each decision type and the language rule for multilingual suppliers here. If the Expertise section is the briefing, Instructions is the playbook.

Additional Context — The rules the agent looks up

This is the reference layer. Static mappings, lookup tables, scoring rubrics, decision logic, and field definitions that the agent consults during execution. It doesn't drive behavior directly — Instructions does that — but it gives the agent the structured data it needs to make the right calls.

In our case, this section held everything that would be too verbose to embed in Instructions: the full decision mapping (Approve / Deny / DenyWithResubmit with priority rules), the answer lookup table mapping qualifiedNumber fields to their meaning, the complete scoring checklists for all four questions, the financial flag thresholds, the compliance flag definitions, and the language rule for comment output. Keeping this separate from Instructions made both sections easier to read, easier to update, and easier to debug when something didn't behave as expected.

TIP💡 A rule of thumb that saved us a lot of iteration: if it describes behavior, it belongs in Instructions. If it describes data or rules the behavior depends on, it belongs in Additional Context. Mixing the two is the most common reason agent configurations become hard to maintain.

Final Thoughts

I want to be honest about something before wrapping this up.

This agent doesn't replace human judgment. It was never meant to. There will always be submissions that land in a grey area — answers that technically check the boxes but still feel incomplete, numbers that pass the threshold but don't quite add up, situations that no rulebook covers cleanly. Those still need a person to look at them, for that we can add a "Human node" on Ariba SLP to make sure a human reviews AI decision.

What the agent does is make sure those are the only cases that reach a human. Everything else — the clean submissions, the straightforward approvals, the obvious denials — gets handled automatically, documented properly, and closed. In most onboarding queues, that's the majority of the work.

What genuinely surprised me wasn't the time I saved, though going from a much longer build down to seven days is hard to dismiss. It was how differently I had to think about the problem.

A few things I'd tell you to pay attention to before you start:

  • Do not rush the destinations. I know it looks like a simple setup step. It isn't. Get the BTP destination and the endpoint actions right before you touch anything else. Every problem I had early on traced back to this. HERE IS WHERE YOU DATA TYPE WILL BE DEFINED.
  • Put the real work into Additional Context. The scoring rubrics, the decision mapping, the language rules — that's where the agent gets smart. A generic configuration gives you a generic agent. The domain-specific rules are what make it actually useful.
  • Test with messy data, not just happy paths. Run a clean approval, a DenyWithResubmit with missing elements, a hard Deny, and at least one Spanish or Portuguese submission. If those four scenarios work correctly, you're in good shape.
  • Always filter on title, not description. The description field can come back empty from the API. I learned this the hard way so you don't have to.

Last but no least let me share some value insights of this PoC
